blocks.content: They stood under the overhang and watched the rain come down.
Tommy Frazier appeared a few doors down under his own overhang, completely dry. He pointed at his hair. Then at theirs. He didn't need to say anything.

blocks.speaker: Eli
blocks.content: He didn't even get wet.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: He left on time.
She said it looking straight at Kit.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Okay. You were right.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: I know.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: You can say it.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: I don't need to say it.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Then what are you going to do with it?

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Hold it. Quietly. Forever.
Eli snorted. Savannah covered her mouth.
Kit leaned against the hardware store wall. His shirt was soaked clean through and there was a piece of wet oak leaf stuck to his collarbone. He reached up and peeled it off.

Daisy saw it. For half a second she'd almost reached up to get it first — the way you do when you've known someone long enough that the distance between their space and yours has gotten blurry — and then she hadn't, because she'd noticed herself almost doing it.
She looked back at the rain.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: You knew it was going to hit, didn't you. You knew before I did.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: I knew when we were still at the park.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Why didn't you say it earlier?

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: I did say it earlier.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: You said it once.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Once should be enough.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: You know me.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: I do know you. That's why I said it twice, actually.
He laughed. The real one.
The rain was still going hard. The street in front of them was a mirror — the streetlights early and amber in the storm-dark, the wet pavement holding all of it.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: I didn't want to go back yet.
He said it without meaning to, really. It just came out.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: I know.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: That obvious?

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: You were watching that sky for twenty minutes.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: I was enjoying the view.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: You were watching it turn green and thinking maybe just five more minutes.
He didn't say anything. She wasn't wrong.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: I didn't want to go back yet either.
He looked at her. She was still looking at the rain, arms crossed against the chill, hair damp, completely at ease with being soaked and mildly right about everything.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: You've been wanting to go up that tower for a while, haven't you.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Two summers.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Two summers and you never said.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: I figured you'd get there eventually.
He looked at her for a second longer than he needed to.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Thanks for coming today.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: You didn't ask us. We just came.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Still.
She looked at him then. Something moved in her expression — something brief and specific — and she turned back to the rain before he could figure out what it was.
The storm was easing. The gutters still ran and the street still gleamed, but the sky to the west had the orange edge coming in behind the clouds.
Almost done.
